images.caption: Figure 1:Validation perplexity versus wall-clock time.PACIreaches the same perplexity levels earlier than 1F1B-flush, showing that removing pipeline bubbles while controlling weight inconsistency translates into improved training time-to-accuracy rather than only higher raw throughput.

Figure 2:Effect of accumulation on forward/backward weight-version inconsistency.
Witha=1a=1(top), several optimizer updates may occur between the forward and backward passes of the same micro-batch.
Witha=4a=4(bottom), parameter versions evolve more slowly, so each micro-batch crosses fewer updates.
Thus, accumulation acts as a version-control mechanism for asynchronous 1F1B.

Figure 3:Relative throughput of 1F1B-flush normalized byPACIas a function of the number of micro-batchesmmfor different micro-batch sizes.The empirical curves closely match the theoretical efficiency predicted by Eq. (3), confirming that the throughput gap is explained by pipeline bubbles.

header: Appendix ADetailed comparison of pipeline parallelism methods

images.caption:
Figure 4:Bubble fraction versus forward/backward inconsistency as the number of micro-batchesmmincreases.
Synchronous 1F1B-flush and interleaved 1F1B use largermmto amortize bubbles, whereasPACIuses accumulation to reduce version drift.
Moderatemmis therefore sufficient for low inconsistency inPACI, but not for high utilization in synchronous schedules.

Figure 5:Training loss versus processed tokens for 1F1B-flush andPACIunder different accumulation factors. Bounded forward/backward inconsistency produces loss trajectories that closely track the synchronous baseline, with no evidence of instability or divergence.

Figure 6:Validation perplexity versus processed tokens for 1F1B-flush andPACIunder different accumulation factors. The trajectories remain closely aligned across configurations, mirroring the training-loss behavior in Figure5and indicating that bounded forward/backward inconsistency does not degrade generalization performance.

Figure 7:Peak GPU memory usage for each micro-batch size (μ\mu) as a function of the number of micro-batches.
Both methods exhibit identical memory usage across all steady-state configurations. The deviation atm=4m=4is due to under-utilization of the pipeline in 1F1B-flush, which prevents reaching steady-state execution.

Figure 8:Throughput as a function of the number of micro-batchesmmfor fixed mini-batch sizesB∈{128,256}B\in\{128,256\}. WhenBBis not divisible bymm, we keep the global mini-batch size fixed and split it into heterogeneous micro-batches: the firstBmodmB\bmod mmicro-batches have size⌊B/m⌋+1\lfloor B/m\rfloor+1, and the remaining micro-batches have size⌊B/m⌋\lfloor B/m\rfloor. Increasingmmreduces the average micro-batch size, leading to a trade-off between improved pipeline utilization for 1F1B-flush and reduced kernel efficiency.PACIremoves bubble overhead and therefore primarily reflects kernel and communication effects, reaching peak performance at moderatemm. Across all configurations,PACIachieves higher throughput, with the gap narrowing at largemmwhere kernel inefficiency dominates.

Figure 9:Throughput as a function of the number of micro-batches for 1F1B-flush andPACI.PACIprovides the largest throughput gains at low-to-moderate micro-batch counts, where pipeline bubbles dominate execution time. As the number of micro-batches increases, the flush baseline becomes increasingly efficient and asymptotically approaches the throughput ofPACI.
